title: The Flight into Egypt
subtitle: By night the Child is carried into exile, and the innocents are slain for His sake

stillness: The lamp is low; the house is asleep.
Wait in the dark with Mary and Joseph.
gospel: When Mary and Joseph had departed, journeying toward Nazareth,
not yet knowing the Lord's counsel concerning this,
behold, an Angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream, saying to him:
Rise, and take the Child and His Mother, and flee into Egypt;
for it shall come to pass that Herod will seek the Child, to destroy Him.
And Joseph, rising, took the Child and His Mother by night,
behold: Behold these things, and meditate upon them, as if you were present.
See how the Mother lifts the sleeping child Jesus,
and the Child, so untimely awakened, weeps;
and have compassion on them, if there is any tenderness in you.
Consider what consolation it could be to the Mother,
when she, a delicate girl newly risen from childbirth,
was driven to flee into a far land, by rough and unknown roads;
when she was forced to carry the tender Child over the long ways;
when she was compelled to dwell among idolaters.
By night they began to go toward the regions of Egypt,
for night befits flight more than day,
that the work might be done more secretly, and the dangers more secretly escaped.
consider: Consider first how the Lord, in His own person, took up adversity as well as prosperity.
At His birth He was magnified by the shepherds, as God;
and a little after He was circumcised, as a sinner.
The Magi came and honored Him greatly;
yet He, remaining in the stable, stood among the beasts, and wept like the son of some poor little man.
Simeon and Anna exalted Him in the temple;
and then the Angel says: flee into Egypt.
As Chrysostom says: the merciful God mingles certain glad things even with sorrowful ones;
He weaves the life of the just out of adversity and prosperity, with a wondrous variety.
When therefore you have consolation, expect tribulation, and the reverse.
We must neither be lifted up in consolations, nor broken in tribulations.
Consider second that the message was given to Joseph, and not to the Mother,
though she was far greater than he:
let the one who receives not prefer himself to the one who receives not;
and let the one who does not receive not be cast down, nor envy the one who does.
Consider third how God permits His own to be vexed by persecutions and tribulations.
Great indeed was the tribulation of the Mother and of Joseph,
when they saw the child Jesus sought for slaughter.
What heavier thing could they hear?
They must go through a far land they did not know, by rough roads, and unfit for the journey:
the Lady for her youth, Joseph for his age, and the Child Himself most tender to carry.
They must dwell as pilgrims in a foreign land, poor, and having as it were nothing.
You too, when you are troubled, have patience;
do not believe a privilege is given you that He gave neither to Himself nor to His Mother.
He fled into Egypt to enlighten and heal its dwellers above the rest:
as Augustine says, where Moses once shut out the day from the faithless,
there Christ, coming, gave light again to those who sat in darkness.
And at Heliopolis they dwelt seven years, as strangers and pilgrims, poor and needy.
The Lady sought what she and her Son needed with distaff and needle;
she sewed and spun, for hire, for love and zeal of poverty.
And what, if sometimes the Son, suffering hunger, asked for bread,
and the Mother had nothing from which to give?
In these things her whole heart was shaken within her.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who, while yet an infant, willed to suffer persecutions and exile,
and willed that infants then be slain for Your sake:
grant to me, wretched one, to bear all these things patiently for You,
and even death, if it must be;
to despise all the prosperity of the world, and to dread none of its adversity.
And you, O most holy Innocents, flowers and first-fruits of the martyrs,
who by innocence and the palm of martyrdom clung to the infant Son of God:
deign, out of the fullness of your grace and innocence,
to obtain for the weakness and misery of my sins
pardon and grace from that same most kind Son of God. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Ecce fugit Christus, et nocte fugit, ut fuga quæ per se difficilis est, noctis obscuritate difficilior fiat.
latinJewel.english: Behold, Christ flees, and He flees by night, so that the flight, which is hard in itself, is made harder still by the darkness of the night.
